title: Reasoning Models
lesson:
Ask a normal language model a hard maths puzzle and it blurts out the first answer that comes to mind sometimes brilliant, sometimes confidently wrong. A reasoning model does something different: before it answers, it thinks out loud to itself. It writes a long private scratchpad of steps "let me work this out... if X then Y... wait, that's wrong, try again..." and only then gives you the final reply. In 2026 the well-known ones are OpenAI's o-series, DeepSeek-R1, Google's Gemini Thinking line, and Anthropic's extended-thinking Claude.

The everyday picture

Analogy one: a quiz show. A normal model is the contestant who slams the buzzer and shouts the first thing in their head. A reasoning model is the student in a maths exam who fills two pages with working before circling the answer. The working isn't decoration it's how they avoid silly mistakes.

Analogy two: System 1 vs System 2 thinking. You recognise your friend's face instantly (fast, automatic). You multiply 17 x 24 slowly, on purpose, step by step (slow, deliberate). Reasoning models are LLMs that have learned to switch into that slow, deliberate gear when a problem needs it.

Where the thinking comes from

You may have met Prompt Engineering tricks like "let's think step by step" that's called chain-of-thought (writing out intermediate reasoning steps instead of jumping to the answer). Reasoning models bake that habit in. They are trained, often with reinforcement learning (the model gets a reward when its final answer is correct, so it learns which kinds of thinking pay off see RLHF), to generate a long internal chain of thought before responding. The big shift around 2024 to 2025 was discovering that letting a model spend more Tokens thinking at answer-time often beats making the model bigger. This is called test-time compute spending effort while answering, not just during training. A neat side effect: the model learns to backtrack. Mid-thought it can notice "that step was wrong" and try a different path, the way you scribble out a wrong line of working rather than building on it.

Those thinking steps are real tokens. They fill the Context Windows (the model's limited working memory) and you pay for them, even though many APIs hide the raw thinking from you and only show a summary plus the final answer.

What it looks like in practice

You don't have to write a clever prompt. You usually just turn thinking on and, on some APIs, dial how hard it thinks:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="o4-2026",
    reasoning_effort="high",   # low | medium | high
    messages=[{"role": "user",
               "content": "A bat and ball cost $1.10. The bat costs $1 more than the ball. How much is the ball?"}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)  # -> "$0.05"  (not the trap answer $0.10)

That bat-and-ball question fools most people and most fast models into saying 10 cents. A reasoning model checks its own arithmetic and lands on 5 cents. The reasoning_effort knob is the practical lever: high thinks longer (slower, pricier, more reliable on hard tasks), low is closer to a normal model.

When to reach for one (and when not to)

Reach for a reasoning modelA normal model is better
Multi-step maths, logic, proofsSimple Q&A, lookups, chit-chat
Hard debugging, planning a multi-file changeRephrasing or summarising text
Puzzles where one wrong step ruins everythingHigh-volume, latency-sensitive calls
The brain inside Agentic WorkflowsCheap classification at scale

The trade is real: thinking burns tokens and time. A reasoning model might take 20 seconds and 10x the tokens to answer something a fast model nails in one second. That's the heart of Latency vs Quality Tradeoffs. For a customer-support FAQ, the wait and cost aren't worth it; for verifying a financial calculation or planning code edits (close cousins of Coding Models), they absolutely are. A common real-world pattern is a two-model setup: a cheap fast model handles the easy 90% of requests and quietly hands the gnarly 10% off to a reasoning model, so you only pay the thinking premium when it earns its keep.

💡 Rule of thumb: if a smart human would need scratch paper, use a reasoning model. If they'd answer instantly, don't pay for thinking you won't use.

One honest caveat: the visible "thinking" is not a perfect window into the model's true process. It's helpful and usually faithful, but treat it as useful working, not a courtroom transcript. The right test is whether the final answers hold up which is exactly what AI Benchmarks measure.
mentalModel: A reasoning model is the exam student who fills two pages with working before circling an answer. It spends extra tokens thinking 'out loud' to itself first, trading speed and cost for far fewer silly mistakes on hard, multi-step problems.
mistakes: Using it for everything. Reasoning costs more tokens, money, and seconds. For simple lookups, summaries, or chit-chat it's pure waste use a fast model and save thinking for genuinely hard problems.
Forgetting thinking tokens count. The internal chain of thought is real tokens that fill the context window and your bill even though many APIs hide the raw thinking and show only a summary.

Adding 'think step by step' on top. Reasoning models already think internally. Bolting on chain-of-thought prompts can confuse them or duplicate effort; give a clear task and let the model do its thing.
Expecting instant replies. A hard query can take many seconds. If you wire one into a live chat UI, stream a 'thinking...' state, or you'll look frozen and frustrate users.
Trusting the visible reasoning as literal truth. The shown 'thinking' is a useful summary, not a guaranteed transcript of the real internal process. Judge the final answer, not the prose around it.
Cranking effort to 'high' by default. Highest effort is slow and expensive and often no better on easy tasks. Start low/medium, measure, and only raise effort where accuracy actually improves.
exercise:
Pick three questions: (1) an easy lookup ('capital of Japan?'), (2) the bat-and-ball puzzle from the lesson, and (3) a small multi-step word problem (e.g. 'A train leaves at 9:40 and arrives at 11:25; a second train is 35 minutes faster how long is its journey?'). Run all three on a fast/normal model and again on a reasoning model (or the same model with thinking turned on). For each, record the answer, whether it's correct, and roughly how long it took. Which questions did thinking actually change the outcome on?

Stretch goal: On the reasoning model, run question 3 at low, medium, and high effort. Note the latency and (if your API reports it) the thinking-token count at each level. Find the lowest effort that still gets the right answer that's your sweet spot for that kind of task.
solution: You should see the fast model and the reasoning model agree on the easy lookup the reasoning one just costs more and takes longer for no gain. On the bat-and-ball and the train problem, the fast model often trips (saying 10 cents, or mis-subtracting the times) while the reasoning model self-corrects and gets them right. That's the whole point: thinking buys accuracy on multi-step problems, nothing on trivial ones. For the stretch goal, you'll usually find medium already nails the train problem, so paying for high adds latency and tokens without improving the answer match effort to difficulty.
summary: Reasoning models write a long private chain of thought before answering, trading extra tokens, time, and cost for big accuracy gains on multi-step maths, logic, planning, and debugging. Many APIs expose a reasoning-effort knob. Use them when a human would need scratch paper, and stick to fast models for simple, high-volume, latency-sensitive work.

keyTerms.def: An LLM trained to generate a long internal chain of thought before its final answer, trading speed/cost for accuracy on hard problems.
Writing out intermediate reasoning steps instead of jumping straight to the answer the habit reasoning models bake in.
Spending extra effort (tokens/time) while answering a question, rather than only during training. More thinking at answer-time can beat a bigger model.
An API setting (often low/medium/high) that controls how long the model thinks before answering higher means slower, pricier, usually more reliable.
The tokens a model spends on its internal reasoning. They consume context and cost money, even when the raw thinking is hidden from you.
A mental model from psychology: fast, automatic thinking vs slow, deliberate thinking. Reasoning models add a 'System 2' gear to LLMs.
Training where the model gets a reward for good outcomes (e.g. a correct final answer) and learns which kinds of thinking earn that reward.
How long the model takes to respond. Reasoning models have higher latency because they generate many thinking tokens first.
